children.name: Hydrochoerus
children.description: Capybaras — the world's largest rodents, semi-aquatic grazers of South American rivers, lakes, and marshes. A large male can weigh 66 kg. Capybaras are highly social, living in groups of 10–20 that may swell to hundreds during the dry season around shrinking water bodies. They are excellent swimmers and can remain submerged for up to five minutes to evade predators. Their eyes, ears, and nostrils are positioned high on the head, allowing them to monitor their surroundings while almost completely submerged. Jaguars, anacondas, caimans, pumas, and harpy eagles all prey on capybaras, making them a keystone prey species across South American wetlands.

children.name: Hydrochoerus hydrochaeris
children.description: The world's largest rodent — up to 1.3 m long and 66 kg. Highly social; their calm temperament and photogenic sociability has made them internet icons. In Venezuela they are farmed for meat under special Catholic dispensations that classify them as fish during Lent.

children.name: Cavia
children.description: Guinea pigs — small, stout rodents of the Andes, domesticated by Andean peoples at least 5,000 years ago for food and still a dietary staple in Peru, Bolivia, and Ecuador. Guinea pigs (called 'cuy') are more efficient at converting plant matter to protein than cattle, and they produce no greenhouse gases. Domestic guinea pigs (Cavia porcellus) descend from wild species still found in the Andes and differ significantly from their wild ancestors in coat colour, body size, and behaviour. Wild guinea pigs are shy and cryptic; domestic ones are placid and tolerant of handling.

children.name: Cavia porcellus
children.description: Domesticated for at least 5,000 years; no wild populations exist. A critical model organism in immunology and nutrition research — 'guinea pig' became synonymous with experimental subject. Highly vocal with a rich repertoire of purrs, rumbles, and squeaks.

children.name: Cavia aperea
children.description: The wild species most closely related to the domestic guinea pig. Lives in dense grassland and forest edge. Much more skittish than domestic animals, fleeing at the slightest disturbance.

children.name: Dolichotis
children.description: Maras (Patagonian cavies) — large, long-legged guinea pig relatives that are among the most unusual rodents on Earth. With their long ears, hare-like legs, and swift bounding gait, they bear a striking resemblance to small deer or jackrabbits, a classic example of convergent evolution. Maras are unusual among rodents in forming long-term monogamous pairs; a male will follow his female closely and drive away rival males. They use a communal crèche system where multiple pairs deposit their young in a shared burrow tended by a rotating group of adults.

children.name: Dolichotis patagonum
children.description: Can reach 45 cm at the shoulder and weigh 9 kg — the fourth largest rodent. Runs at up to 45 km/h. Population declining due to livestock grazing, habitat loss, and hunting; near threatened.
